title: Tracking Involuntary vs Voluntary Churn in Stripe (What Actually Works)
meta_description: Stripe lumps all cancellations together. Here's how to actually separate involuntary churn from voluntary churn without building your own ETL pipeline.
h1: How to Track Involuntary Churn Separately From Voluntary Churn in Stripe
body_markdown:
A founder I know lost $1,840 in MRR in March and spent a week convinced his product was broken. It wasn't. Seven customers had expired cards, two had hit their Visa auto-decline threshold after a BIN update, and one was a legitimate cancel because they'd been acquired. He'd been staring at the Stripe Billing dashboard's churn number — a single bar on a single chart — treating it like one signal when it was actually two very different problems glued together.

This is the whole reason you need to track involuntary churn separately from voluntary churn in Stripe. They have nothing in common. One is a product problem. The other is a payments problem. Fixing them requires different playbooks, different people, and different tools — and if you look at them as one number, you'll chase the wrong one for months.

I did. I spent most of H2 2023 A/B testing onboarding copy while my real issue was that Stripe Radar was silently blocking renewals from a handful of corporate AmEx cards. Onboarding was fine. My dunning was the problem. Six months of wrong diagnosis.

So let's get into what actually separates these two, why Stripe makes it weirdly hard, and which of the five tools I tested is worth the money.

## Why Stripe's default churn number is basically useless

Stripe defines a cancellation as a subscription transitioning to `canceled` status. Fine. But that status gets set in at least four different ways, and Stripe's dashboard does not, by default, tell you which path a given cancellation took.

Here are the paths:

- The customer hit the cancel button in your app, which called `subscriptions.cancel` immediately.
- The customer set `cancel_at_period_end = true` and rode it out.
- Stripe Smart Retries exhausted its retry schedule and flipped the sub to `canceled` (or `unpaid`, depending on your settings in Billing > Subscriptions > Manage failed payments).
- You, the operator, canceled it manually because the customer emailed you.

The first two are voluntary. The third is involuntary. The fourth is ambiguous and honestly a mess — I'll come back to that.

In Stripe's own dashboard, under Billing > Revenue recovery, you *can* see recovered revenue and failed payment stats. That's useful. But there's no view that says "here is your involuntary churn rate this month, here is your voluntary churn rate this month, plotted against each other." You have to reconstruct it.

The reconstruction logic isn't that hard conceptually. For each canceled subscription, look at the final invoice. If the final invoice has `status = uncollectible` or the subscription entered `past_due` before cancellation, it's involuntary. If the final invoice was paid and the subscription went from `active` → `canceled` (or `active` → `cancel_at_period_end` → `canceled`), it's voluntary.

The problem is doing this at scale, every day, without writing a pipeline you then have to maintain forever.

## The five tools I tried, ranked honestly

I spent most of Q1 2024 evaluating tools for this exact problem. I'm going to name them and tell you what I actually think, because every comparison post I read was diplomatic mush.

**ChartMogul** gets this right. Their "Customer Churn" view has a dedicated filter for "delinquent" vs voluntary cancellations, and their reason-code tagging is the most granular of anything I tried. The $129/mo Launch plan covers most bootstrappers. The downside: their definition of "delinquent" is their own, not Stripe's, so if you're reconciling against Stripe's Revenue Recovery report you'll see small discrepancies. Annoying but not fatal.

**Baremetrics** technically separates the two but buries it. You have to go to Customers > Cancellations, then filter by "Involuntary." There's no side-by-side chart in the default dashboard. Also, Baremetrics' Startup plan jumped from $58 to $129/mo somewhere around 2022 and the involuntary-churn tooling didn't meaningfully improve in that window. I don't love it.

**ProfitWell (now Paddle Retain)** — here's where I get grumpy. Retain is pitched specifically as an involuntary churn recovery product, which is fine, but the analytics side got worse after the Paddle acquisition in 2022. The free Metrics product still works, but the involuntary churn dashboard is shallow and the card-updater service they upsell is now bundled weirdly. I'd skip it unless you're already a Paddle customer.

**Putler** is cheap ($29/mo last I checked) and shows a combined churn number with no involuntary breakdown worth mentioning. Not for this job.

**Orb** and similar usage-billing platforms are overkill if you're just trying to see two numbers. Don't even start there unless you're doing metered billing.

If I had to pick one tool for a solo founder doing $5k-$30k MRR, I'd pick ChartMogul and I'd do it today.

But you can also just build this yourself, and for a lot of founders that's the right move.

## The DIY version that actually works

Here's the approach I ended up using before I gave in and paid for tooling. It's not elegant. It works.

Set up a webhook endpoint listening for `customer.subscription.deleted`. When the event fires, pull the subscription object. Check two things: the `cancellation_details.reason` field (Stripe added this in their 2022 Billing update and it's genuinely useful), and the status of the most recent invoice.

The `cancellation_details.reason` field can be `cancellation_requested`, `payment_disputed`, `payment_failed`, or a few others. `payment_failed` is your involuntary signal. `cancellation_requested` is voluntary. Dispute-driven cancellations I treat as their own bucket because they behave differently (and usually mean something went wrong earlier in the customer lifecycle).

Dump each canceled sub into a Google Sheet or a tiny Postgres table with columns for: customer ID, MRR lost, cancellation reason, date, and whether the last invoice was paid. Then build two simple counts per month. Voluntary churn rate = voluntary cancellations / active subs at start of month. Involuntary churn rate = involuntary cancellations / active subs at start of month.

That's it. That's the whole system.

I ran this for about nine months on a $18k MRR business and it was enough to catch the Radar-blocking-AmEx issue I mentioned earlier. Once I saw involuntary churn spike from its usual ~0.8%/mo to 2.3% in a single month, I knew to look at payment failures instead of product.

One warning: do not trust `cancellation_details.reason` retroactively. It only gets populated on subs canceled after you've set up the Billing portal correctly, and older subs in your account will have `null` there. I got this wrong at first and spent a weekend convinced my webhook was broken.

## What to actually do with the two numbers once you have them

This is the part nobody writes about, probably because it requires having an opinion.

If your involuntary churn is above ~1.5% of MRR per month, your problem is dunning, not product. In rough order of impact: turn on Stripe's Smart Retries if you haven't (under Billing settings), enable the card account updater for Visa/Mastercard (it's a checkbox, it costs nothing extra on most Stripe plans, and Andrew Chen wrote about this being one of the highest-ROI 10-minute changes a SaaS founder can make — I think that was 2019 on his blog but the advice is still correct), and write three dunning emails that actually sound like a human wrote them. Not "Your payment has failed." Something like "Hey, your card got declined — here's a one-click link to update it."

Patrick McKenzie's old line about this, roughly:

> "The difference between a 2% and a 0.5% involuntary churn rate over five years is the difference between a company and a hobby."

He's right. Compounding works in both directions.

If your voluntary churn is the bigger number — and for most SaaS under $50k MRR, it is — then involuntary churn optimization is a distraction. Go do customer interviews. Read the cancellation reasons customers type into your cancel flow (you do have a cancel flow that asks why, right?). Look for patterns. The answer is almost never "lower the price." It's usually "the product didn't do the one thing they thought it would do."

The one place these two metrics intersect: customers who churn involuntarily *were going to churn anyway* a disproportionate amount of the time. A customer who's actively using your product will update their card. A customer who forgot they were paying you will let the card expire. So your involuntary churn number has a voluntary-churn ghost hiding inside it, and no tool I've tested separates that out cleanly. You have to look at usage data alongside it.

Which brings me to the annoying honest answer: tracking these two metrics separately is the easy part. Acting on them is the hard part, and no dashboard — mine included — is going to do that for you.

If you want the numbers without the webhook-plumbing weekend, [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) pulls them apart automatically and shows you both rates in one view, which is roughly what I wish I'd had in March 2023 instead of a canceled Saturday and a suspicion that my product was falling apart. It wasn't. It was just a BIN update.
target_keyword: track involuntary churn separately from voluntary churn stripe
cover_image_prompt: Editorial illustration, dark mode, deep navy and charcoal background. Two abstract branching rivers of light flowing downward and diverging — one cool teal, one muted amber — representing two types of customer churn splitting from a single source. Thin geometric lines, minimal negative space, subtle grid pattern in the background suggesting a financial ledger. A single small circular node at the top where both rivers originate. Premium tech magazine aesthetic, concept-art style, no text, no people, no logos. Soft volumetric lighting, slight grain texture.
